frameworks.name: Bayesian model selection
frameworks.one_line: Marginal-likelihood integration over latent causes penalizes excessive model flexibility.

frameworks.name: Occam's razor
frameworks.one_line: Simpler explanations are preferred when fit to data is comparable.

frameworks.name: artificial neural networks
frameworks.one_line: ANNs fail to show the same simplicity preference even when adaptive.

core_question: Do humans prefer simpler explanations for uncertain data in a way that matches formal model-selection theories?
key_assumptions: Decision-making can be cast as choosing among alternative explanatory models.
Model selection should integrate over all relevant explanations, not just the best fit.
Flexibility penalties emerge naturally from marginal-likelihood computation.
manipulations: Pairs of alternative explanations differing in complexity for the same uncertain data.
Conditions where simplicity preference is adaptive vs. maladaptive.
Human participants vs. artificial neural network agents on matched tasks.
key_findings: People prefer the simpler of two alternative explanations for uncertain data.
Human preferences match predictions of formal model-selection theories that penalize flexibility.
Simplicity preference persists in humans even when maladaptive.
Certain artificial neural networks do not show the same persistent simplicity preference.
interpretations: Principled statistical model selection — integrating over latent causes to avoid overfitting — may play a central role in human decision-making.
Flexibility penalties guiding human choice arise from considering the integral over all relevant explanations.
